revision.text:
[[ar:دايكوكوتين]][[bcl:Daikokuten]][[de:Daikoku]][[en:Daikokuten]][[es:Daikokuten]][[fa:دایکوکوتن]][[fi:Daikokuten]][[fr:Daikokuten]][[he:דאיקוקו]][[id:Daikokuten]][[it:Daikokuten]][[ja:大黒天]][[ko:대흑천]][[pl:Daikoku]][[ru:Дайкокутэн]][[simple:Daikokuten]][[sv:Daikoku]][[ta:டைகோகுடென்]][[tr:Daikokuten]] {{Short description|Japanese deity of fortune and wealth|qq=}} {{Infobox deity | type = Buddhist | name = Daikokuten | deity_of = God of wealth, fortune, the household, agriculture, fertility, sexuality and war | member_of = the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] | image = Descriptive catalogue of the collection of Buddhist art in the United States National museum (1921) (20863366652).jpg | alt = | caption = | other_names = Makakara (摩訶迦羅)
Makakaraten (摩訶迦羅天)
Daikokutenjin (大黒天神)
Daikokusonten (大黒尊天)
Daikoku-[[Japanese honorifics#San|san]] (大黒さん)
Daikoku-[[Japanese honorifics#Sama|sama]] (大黒様 / 大黒さま) | script_name = Japanese | script = 大黒天 | affiliation = {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}
{{ill|Mahakala|en|Mahakala|qid=Q922824|lt=Mahakala|1=Mahakala}} (prototype)
[[Ōkuninushi]] (conflated with) | mantra = ''Oṃ Mahākālāya [[:en:Svāhā|Svāhā]]''
(''On Makakyaraya sowaka'') | symbols = sack, {{ill|Uchide no kozuchi|en|Uchide no kozuchi|qid=Q7876713|lt=mallet|1=Uchide no kozuchi}}, rice bales | animals = {{ill|Mouse|en|Mouse|qid=Q2751034|lt=Mouse|1=Mouse}} or {{ill|Rat|en|Rat|qid=Q26018|lt=Rat|1=Rat}} | Hinduism_equivalent = {{ill|Shiva|en|Shiva|qid=Q11378|lt=Shiva|1=Shiva}}, {{ill|Kubera|en|Kubera|qid=Q223617|lt=Kubera|1=Kubera}} }} '''Daikokuten''' () is a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic]] {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese deity of fortune and wealth. Daikokuten originated from {{ill|Mahakala|en|Mahakala|qid=Q922824|lt=Mahakala|1=Mahakala}}, the Buddhist version of the {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hindu|1=Hinduism}} {{ill|Deva (Hinduism)|en|Deva (Hinduism)|qid=Q211258|lt=deity|1=Deva (Hinduism)}} {{ill|Shiva|en|Shiva|qid=Q11378|lt=Shiva|1=Shiva}}, conflated with the native [[Shinto]] god [[Ōkuninushi]].{{cite book|first=Jeremy|last=Roberts|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|publisher=Infobase Publishing|year=2009|page=28|isbn=9781438128023|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC|qq=}}{{cite book|first=Pratapaditya|last=Pal|title=Indian Sculpture: 700-1800|publisher=Los Angeles County Museum of Art|page=180|qq=}} ===Mahākāla in East Asian Buddhism=== {{see also|Shiva|Mahākāla|Maheśvara (Buddhism)|qq=}} [[File:MET DP314097.jpg|thumb|250px|left|11th–12th century stone stele of Mahākāla from {{ill|Bihar|en|Bihar|qid=Q1165|lt=Bihar|1=Bihar}} ({{ill|Metropolitan Museum of Art|en|Metropolitan Museum of Art|qid=Q160236|lt=Metropolitan Museum of Art|1=Metropolitan Museum of Art}})]] The {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} term 'Mahākāla' ("Great Black [One]", "Great Time" or "Great Death"{{cite book |last1=Jain |first1=Jyotindra |title=Picture Showmen: Insights Into the Narrative Tradition in Indian Art |date=1998 |publisher=Marg Publications |page=34 |isbn=9788185026398 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nxLaAAAAMAAJ|qq=}}) was originally one of the epithets of the {{ill|Hinduism|en|Hinduism|qid=Q9089|lt=Hindu|1=Hinduism}} god {{ill|Shiva|en|Shiva|qid=Q11378|lt=Shiva|1=Shiva}} in his aspect as {{ill|Time|en|Time|qid=Q11471|lt=Time|1=Time}} (''kāla''), the ultimate destroyer of all things.{{cite book |last1=Finegan |first1=Jack |title=An Archaeological History of Religions of Indian Asia |date=1989 |publisher=Paragon House |page=143 |isbn=9780913729434 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BrDXAAAAMAAJ|qq=}} This title and aspect of Shiva was eventually adopted by {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, where Mahākāla became reinterpreted as a ''[[:en:Dharmapāla|Dharmapāla]]'' or a protector of the Buddhist {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}} but also as a terrifying deity who roams the forests at night with {{ill|Gana|en|Gana|qid=Q632279|lt=hordes|1=Gana}} of ghouls and demons in his train.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=45–46|qq=}} Mahākāla is mentioned in many {{ill|Chinese Buddhist canon|en|Chinese Buddhist canon|qid=Q17023673|lt=Chinese Buddhist texts|1=Chinese Buddhist canon}}, although iconographic depictions of him in {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} were rare during the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang|1=Tang dynasty}} and {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song|1=Song dynasty}} periods. He eventually became the center of a flourishing cult after the 9th century in the kingdoms of {{ill|Nanzhao|en|Nanzhao|qid=Q1045322|lt=Nanzhao|1=Nanzhao}} and {{ill|Dali Kingdom|en|Dali Kingdom|qid=Q26472|lt=Dali|1=Dali Kingdom}} in what is now the province of {{ill|Yunnan|en|Yunnan|qid=Q43194|lt=Yunnan|1=Yunnan}}, a region bordering {{ill|Tibet|en|Tibet|qid=Q17252|lt=Tibet|1=Tibet}}, where his cult was also widespread. Due to Tibetan influence, his importance further increased during the {{ill|Mongol|en|Mongol|qid=Q55661099|lt=Mongol|1=Mongols (disambiguation)}}-led {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|qid=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty|1=Yuan dynasty}}, with his likeness being displayed in the imperial palace and in Buddhist temples inside and outside the [[:en:Khanbaliq|capital]] (though most of these images are now no longer extant).{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=46–47|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Howard |first1=Angela Falco |last2=Li |first2=Song |last3=Wu |first3=Hung |last4=Yang |first4=Hong |title=Chinese Sculpture |date=2006 |publisher=Yale University Press |page=416 |isbn=9780300100655 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PGuPsNCaJdwC&dq=mahakala+chinese&pg=PA416|qq=}} The deity's name was both {{ill|Transcription into Chinese characters|en|Transcription into Chinese characters|qid=Q1202199|lt=transcribed|1=Transcription into Chinese characters}} into [[:en:Hanzi|Chinese characters]] as {{Lang|zh|摩訶迦羅|qq=}} ({{lang-zh|p=Móhējiāluó|qq=}}; {{ill|Middle Chinese|en|Middle Chinese|qid=Q2016252|lt=Middle Chinese|1=Middle Chinese}} ({{ill|Baxter's transcription for Middle Chinese|en|Baxter's transcription for Middle Chinese|qid=Q4873818|lt=Baxter|1=Baxter's transcription for Middle Chinese}}): ''mwa xa kæ la'') and {{ill|Chinese translation theory|en|Chinese translation theory|qid=Q5100951|lt=translated|1=Chinese translation theory}} as {{Lang|zh|大黑天|qq=}} ({{lang-zh|p=Dàhēitiān|l=Great Black {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}|qq=}}, with ''kāla'' being understood to mean 'black'; M. C. (Baxter): ''dɑH xok then''). These were eventually {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|qid=Q4222379|lt=adopted|1=Sino-Japanese vocabulary}} into {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} as ''Makakara'' (or ''Makakyara'') and ''Daikokuten'', respectively. [[File:Hangzhou Baochengsi Mahegela Zaoxiang 20120518-07.jpg|thumb|320px|Mahākāla (center) flanked by the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}s {{ill|Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)|en|Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)|qid=Q868306|lt=Samantabhadra|1=Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)}} (left) and [[:en:Mañjuśrī|Mañjuśrī]] (right). Baocheng Temple, {{ill|Hangzhou|en|Hangzhou|qid=Q4970|lt=Hangzhou|1=Hangzhou}}, {{ill|Zhejiang|en|Zhejiang|qid=Q16967|lt=Zhejiang|1=Zhejiang}}, {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}]] In some texts, Mahākāla is described as a fearsome god, a "demon who steals the vital essence (of people)" and who feeds on flesh and blood, though he is also said to only devour those who committed sins against the [[:en:Refuge (Buddhism)|Three Jewels]] of Buddhism.{{cite web |last1=Schumacher |first1=Mark |title=Daikokuten Iconography in Japan |url=https://www.onmarkproductions.com/Daikokuten/ |website=A-to-Z Photo Dictionary of Japanese Religious Sculpture and Art (www.onmarkproductions.com) |access-date=2021-04-21|qq=}} One story found in the Tang-era monk [[Yi Xing]]'s commentary on the ''[[:en:Mahavairocana Tantra|Mahāvairocana Tantra]]'' portrays Mahākāla as a manifestation of the {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=buddha|1=Buddhahood}} {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}} who subjugated the [[Dakini|ḍākinīs]], a race of flesh-eating female demons, by swallowing them. Mahākāla released them on the condition that they no longer kill humans, decreeing that they could only eat the {{ill|Heart|en|Heart|qid=Q1072|lt=Heart|1=Heart}} – believed to contain the vital essence of humans known as 'human yellow' ({{Lang|zh|人黄|qq=}}, {{lang-zh|p=rénhuáng|qq=}}, Japanese: ''jin'ō'') – of those who were near death.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=The Fluid Pantheon: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 1 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |page=195|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=117–118|qq=}}{{cite web |title=荼枳尼天 (Dakiniten) |url=http://tobifudo.jp/newmon/shinbutu/dagini.html |website=Flying Deity Tobifudō (Ryūkō-zan Shōbō-in Official Website) |access-date=2021-04-08|qq=}} A tale found in {{ill|Amoghavajra|en|Amoghavajra|qid=Q1363621|lt=Amoghavajra|1=Amoghavajra}}'s translation of the ''{{ill|Humane King Sutra|en|Humane King Sutra|qid=Q5939265|lt=Humane King Sūtra|1=Humane King Sutra}}'' relates how a heterodox (i.e. non-Buddhist) master instructed {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|qid=Q17006691|lt=Prince Kalmāṣapāda|1=Kalmashapada}} (斑足王) to offer the heads of a thousand kings to Mahākāla, the "great black god of the graveyard" ({{Lang|zh|塚間摩訶迦羅大黑天神|qq=}}), if he wished to ascend the throne of his kingdom.{{cite web |title=仁王護國般若波羅蜜多經 護国品第五 - T. 0246|url=https://21dzk.l.u-tokyo.ac.jp/SAT2018/T0246_.08.0840b07.html |website=SAT Daizokyo Text Database |access-date=2021-04-21|author=Amoghavajra|qq=}}{{cite journal |last1=Iyanaga |first1=Nobumi |title=Under the Shadow of the Great Śiva: Tantric Buddhism and its Influence on Japanese Mediaeval Culture|url=https://www.academia.edu/39725624 |website=academia.edu |access-date=2021-04-21 |date=2008|qq=}} [[File:Sichuan o yunnan, mahakala, guardiano della dottrina, xiv sec con piedistallo del xvi sec.JPG|left|thumb|{{ill|Ming dynasty|en|Ming dynasty|qid=Q9903|lt=Ming dynasty|1=Ming dynasty}} statue of Dàhēitiān. {{ill|Sichuan|en|Sichuan|qid=Q19770|lt=Sichuan|1=Sichuan}}, {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}. 14th century (with pedestal from the 16th century)]] As time went by, Mahākāla also became seen as a guardian of {{ill|Vihāra|en|Vihāra|qid=Q844619|lt=Buddhist monasteries|1=Vihāra}}, especially its kitchens. The monk {{ill|Yijing (monk)|en|Yijing (monk)|qid=Q457629|lt=Yijing|1=Yijing (monk)}}, who traveled to {{ill|Srivijaya|en|Srivijaya|qid=Q234197|lt=Srivijaya|1=Srivijaya}} and India during the late 7th century, claimed that images of Mahākāla were to be found in the kitchens and porches of Indian Buddhist monasteries, before which offerings of food were made:{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |page=49|qq=}} {{quote|There is likewise in great monasteries in India, at the side of a pillar in the kitchen, or before the porch, a figure of a deity carved in wood, two or three feet high, holding a golden bag, and seated on a small chair, with one foot hanging down towards the ground. Being always wiped with oil its countenance is blackened, and the deity is called Mahākāla [莫訶哥羅, {{lang-zh|p=Mòhēgēluō|qq=}}, M.C. (Baxter): ''mak xa ka la''] or the great black deity [大黑神, {{lang-zh|p=Dàhēishén|qq=}}, M. C. (Baxter): ''dɑH xok zyin'']. The ancient tradition asserts that he belonged to the beings (in the heaven) of the great god (or [[:en:Maheśvara (Buddhism)|Maheśvara]]). He naturally loves the Three Jewels, and protects the five assemblies from misfortune. Those who offer prayers to him have their desires fulfilled. At meal-times those who serve in the kitchen offer light and incense, and arrange all kinds of prepared food before the deity. (...) In China the image of that deity has often been found in the districts of {{ill|Jiangnan|en|Jiangnan|qid=Q1367898|lt=Kiang-nan|1=Jiangnan}}, though not in {{ill|Huaibei|en|Huaibei|qid=Q360268|lt=Huai-poh|1=Huaibei}}. Those who ask him (for a boon) find their wishes fulfilled. The efficacy of that deity is undeniable.{{cite book |author1=I-Tsing |author1-link=Yijing (monk) |title=A Record of the Buddhist Religion as Practised in India and the Malay Archipelago |date=1896 |publisher=Clarendon Press |location=Oxford |pages=38–39 |url=https://archive.org/details/recordofbuddhist00ichi/page/38/mode/2up|translator=J. Takakusu|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |chapter=卷第一 |wslink= 南海寄歸內法傳|title=南海寄歸內法傳 |wslanguage=zh|author=義淨|qq=}}|qq=}} Yijing then relates an anecdote about how the deity once miraculously provided food for five hundred monks who came to visit the monastery of Makuṭabandhana in {{ill|Kushinagar|en|Kushinagar|qid=Q825673|lt=Kushinagar|1=Kushinagar}} after one of the female servants prayed and made offerings before his image. This idea of Mahākāla as one who brought prosperity to monasteries and granted wishes may have contributed to the identification of the deity as a god of wealth and fortune in Japan. In China, the god was also associated with fertility and sexuality: during the {{ill|Qixi Festival|en|Qixi Festival|qid=Q13423122|lt=Qixi Festival|1=Qixi Festival}} (a.k.a. the Double Seventh Festival) held on the 7th day of the 7th month of the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|qid=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}, married women traditionally bought dolls or figurines called 'Móhéluó' ({{Lang|zh|魔合羅|qq=}}) or 'Móhóuluó' ({{Lang|zh|摩睺羅|qq=}}) – the term probably deriving from 'Mahākāla' – in the hopes of giving birth to a child.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=52–53|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Johnson |first1=Dale |title=A Glossary of Words and Phrases in the Oral Performing and Dramatic Literatures of the Jin, Yuan, and Ming |date=2021 |publisher=University of Michigan Press |page=157 |isbn=9780472038237 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=olP7DwAAQBAJ|qq=}}{{cite book |editor1-last=Hsia |editor1-first=Chih-tsing |editor2-last=Kao |editor2-first=George |editor3-last=Li |editor3-first=Wai-yee |title=The Columbia Anthology of Yuan Drama |date=2014 |publisher=Columbia University Press |page=147 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gM0YBQAAQBAJ |chapter=The Moheluo Doll (Meng Hanqing)|isbn=9780231122672 |translator=Jonathan Chaves|qq=}} Ritual texts also prescribe the worship of Mahākāla to women looking for a male partner or to pregnant women. ===Transformation in Japan=== {{see also|Ōkuninushi|Ōmononushi|qq=}} [[File:Daikokuten (from the Besson Zakki).jpg|thumb|Daikokuten (from the ''Besson Zakki'')]] Upon being introduced to Japan via the esoteric {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} and {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} sects, Mahākāla (as 'Daikokuten') gradually transformed into a jovial, beneficent figure as his positive qualities (such as being the purveyor of wealth and fertility) increasingly came to the fore – mostly at the expense of his darker traits. Whereas earlier images of Daikokuten showed him as wrathful (or at least stern-faced), later artworks consistently came to portray him as smiling.{{cite web |last1=Iwai |first1=Hiroshi |title=Daikokuten |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=203 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2021-04-12|qq=}} [[Saichō]], the founder of the Tendai school, is credited with bringing the cult of Mahākāla-Daikokuten to Japan. Legend claims that when he first climbed {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} (located northeast of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}), Mahākāla appeared to him in the form of an old man and offered to become the guardian of the monastic community envisioned by Saichō, what would become known as [[Enryaku-ji]].{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=50–52|qq=}} By the medieval period, when Buddhism and native Japanese beliefs ([[Shinto]]) were becoming [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretized]], Daikokuten became conflated with the native ''[[kami]]'' [[Ōkuninushi]] (大国主), as the first two characters of the latter's name (大国) [[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|can also be read as]] 'Daikoku'. Daikokuten's status as patron of Enryaku-ji also influenced this connection: he was identified with [[:ja:山王権現|Sannō Gongen]], the deity enshrined in {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|qid=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} at the eastern foot of Mount Hiei, who in turn was identified with Ōkuninushi or [[Ōmononushi]] (Miwa {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|qid=Q5369590|lt=Myōjin|1=Myōjin}}, the god of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} who is also interpreted as Ōkuninushi under another name or an aspect of his). [[File:Shirousagi_-02.jpg|thumb|left|310px|The god [[Ōkuninushi]], bearing a sack, meeting the [[Hare of Inaba]]]] The sack or bag Daikokuten carries (already attested in Yijing's description of portrayals of Mahākāla in India) served to further associate the god with Ōkuninushi: in the story of the [[Hare of Inaba]] (found in the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}}), the young Ōkuninushi is said to have originally been treated by his wicked elder brothers as their luggage carrier.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |page=54|qq=}}{{cite web |title=因幡の白兎を救った大黒様が抱えていた袋の中身 |url=https://www.hotel-nagata.co.jp/2014/05/10/13948 |website=ホテルながた (Hotel Nagata) |access-date=2021-04-10|qq=}}{{cite web |title=コラム17 大黒様と俵 |url=http://www.kumagaya-bunkazai.jp/museum/dokusyo/koramu/daikokusama/daikokuten.htm |website=Kumagaya Digital Museum |access-date=2021-04-10|qq=}} Besides the sack, Daikokuten began to acquire other attributes such as the golden [[mallet]] called ''{{ill|Uchide no kozuchi|en|Uchide no kozuchi|qid=Q7876713|lt=Uchide no kozuchi|1=Uchide no kozuchi}}'' (lit. "tap-appear little mallet", i.e. a mallet that strikes out anything the user desires) and two big bales of {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}. He was also considered a [[:en:God of fertility|God of fertility]], and was thus also portrayed making the obscene {{ill|Fig sign|en|Fig sign|qid=Q196006|lt=Fig sign|1=Fig sign}}, carrying a suggestively bifurcated {{ill|Daikon|en|Daikon|qid=Q7224757|lt=Daikon|1=Daikon}} (sometimes called the "bride of Daikoku"), sporting a huge [[:en:Erect penis|Erect penis]], or being entirely represented himself by a wooden phallus.{{cite book |last1=Turnbull |first1=Stephen |title=Japan's Sexual Gods: Shrines, Roles and Rituals of Procreation and Protection |date=2015 |publisher=Brill |isbn=978-9004293786|pages=244–245|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Chaudhuri |first1=Saroj Kumar |title=Hindu Gods and Goddesses in Japan |date=2003 |publisher=Vedams eBooks (P) Ltd |isbn=978-8179360095|pages=70–71|qq=}} {{ill|Mouse|en|Mouse|qid=Q2751034|lt=Mice|1=Mouse}} and {{ill|Rat|en|Rat|qid=Q26018|lt=Rat|1=Rat}}s also became a part of Daikokuten's iconography, due to Mahākāla's association with {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Vaiśravaṇa|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} (''Bishamonten'' in Japanese), the Buddhist analogue to the Hindu {{ill|Kubera|en|Kubera|qid=Q223617|lt=Kubera|1=Kubera}}, and {{ill|Pañcika|en|Pañcika|qid=Q7157069|lt=Pañcika|1=Pañcika}}, Vaiśravaṇa's general and consort of the {{ill|Yaksha|en|Yaksha|qid=Q6011093|lt=yakshini|1=Yaksha}} goddess [[:en:Hārītī|Hārītī]] (known in Japan as ''Kishimojin''), who were both associated with the northern direction – which corresponds to the sign of the {{ill|Rat (zodiac)|en|Rat (zodiac)|qid=Q721997|lt=Rat|1=Rat (zodiac)}} in the {{ill|Chinese zodiac|en|Chinese zodiac|qid=Q1976204|lt=Chinese zodiac|1=Chinese zodiac}}. (One of the twelve {{ill|Guardians of the directions|en|Guardians of the directions|qid=Q1398903|lt=dikpālas|1=Guardians of the directions}} or guardians of the directions in Buddhism is {{ill|Ishana|en|Ishana|qid=Q608366|lt=Īśāna|1=Ishana}}, the guardian of the northeast who, like Mahākāla, is a Buddhicized form of Shiva.) This also contributed to the conflation of Daikokuten with Ōkuninushi, as mice [[Ōkuninushi#Ōnamuji and Suseribime|also figured in the latter's mythology]].{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=45–46, 366|qq=}} [[File:恵比寿と大黒天 - Ebisu and Daikoku.jpg|thumb|315px|18th century ''[[ukiyo-e]]'' depicting Daikokuten (left) and {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} (right)]] Medieval exegetes interpreted Mahākāla-Daikokuten in both a positive and a negative way: on the one hand he was seen as a symbol of fundamental {{ill|Avidyā (Buddhism)|en|Avidyā (Buddhism)|qid=Q1053923|lt=ignorance|1=Avidyā (Buddhism)}} (expressed by the name 'Daikoku', which can be interpreted as "great darkness"), but on the other hand he also represented the [[:en:Hongaku|nonduality]] of ignorance (symbolized by the character 黒, 'black(ness) / dark(ness)') and {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|qid=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}} (designated by the character 大, 'great'). He was identified with [[:ja:一字金輪仏頂|Ichiji Kinrin]] (Ekākṣaroṣṇīṣacakra, a manifestation of both the cosmic buddha Vairocana – specifically, Vairocana's head knob or ''{{ill|Ushnisha|en|Ushnisha|qid=Q688924|lt=uṣṇīṣa|1=Ushnisha}}'' – and the sacred syllable ''bhrūṃ'') and thus a symbol of ultimate reality, but also with the directional deity Īśāna (who as noted earlier was another deity derived from Shiva), who is also considered to be a god of obstacles. Indeed, because of the stigma related to his origins, he was identified in some texts as a ''jissha'' (実者, lit. "true/real one", also known as 実類, ''jitsurui''), a 'real' god considered inferior to deities who are provisional [[Honji suijaku|manifestations]] of enlightened buddhas and bodhisattvas (''[[gongen]]''). However, medieval esoteric Buddhism also posited the existence of a 'higher' Daikokuten, the conventional Daikokuten being but one of the various guises he takes. While the latter represented ignorance, the former was seen as transmuting ignorance into awakening.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=55–56|qq=}} Daikokuten was also linked or identified with other deities such as [[Ugajin]], [[Benzaiten]] (the Buddhist version of [[:en:Sarasvatī|Sarasvatī]]), Vaiśravana-Bishamonten, the earth god [[:ja:堅牢地神|Kenrō Jijin]] (derived from the Indian earth goddess [[:en:Pṛthivī|Pṛthivī]], though the deity is also portrayed in Japan as male{{cite web |title=堅牢地神（地天） |url=https://www.butuzou.co.jp/syotenjin/kenrouchijin.html |website=Butsuzō Museum (仏像美術館) |access-date=2021-04-11|qq=}}), or the [[Wisdom King|wisdom king]] [[Acala]] (''Fudō Myōō'' in Japanese). Indeed, Acala, like Mahākāla-Daikokuten, is credited in some sources with defeating and converting the ḍākinīs and is also considered to be a wrathful avatar of Vairocana. (Likewise, Acala is also thought by some scholars to be derived in one way or another from Shiva.){{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=The Fluid Pantheon: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 1 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=136. 195|qq=}} In popular belief, Daikokuten is also commonly paired with the folk deity {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}. Just as Daikokuten was conflated with Ōkuninushi, Ebisu was sometimes identified with Ōkuninushi's son [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]{{cite web |title=えびす様と だいこく様 |url=https://www.tooka-ebisu.or.jp/gods/ |website=十日恵比須神社 (Tōka Ebisu Shrine Official Website) |access-date=2021-04-12|qq=}} or the dwarf god [[Sukunabikona]], who assisted Ōkuninushi in developing the land of Japan.{{cite web |title=神田明神とは |url=https://www.kandamyoujin.or.jp/profile/ |website=Kanda Shrine (Kanda Myōjin) Official Website |access-date=2021-04-18 |language=Japanese|qq=}} In homes, the two deities were enshrined in the kitchen or oven, while merchants worshiped them as patron deities of commercial success. Farmers meanwhile revered them as gods of the [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] (''[[Ta-no-Kami|ta-no-kami]]''). ==Iconography== [[File:大黒天（大悲胎藏大曼荼羅 仁和寺版） - Mahakala (Ninna-ji Taizō Mandala).jpg|left|thumb|215px|Mahākāla as depicted in the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|qid=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm|1=Womb Realm}} (''Garbhadhātu'') [[Mandala|Maṇḍala]], holding {{ill|Gajasurasamhara|en|Gajasurasamhara|qid=Q12976075|lt=an elephant hide|1=Gajasurasamhara}}, a sword, a human and a goat]] [[File:Andhakasuravadha.jpg|thumb|The iconography of the Buddhist Mahākāla is thought to be based on the mythic episode of {{ill|Shiva|en|Shiva|qid=Q11378|lt=Shiva|1=Shiva}} spearing the demon {{ill|Andhaka|en|Andhaka|qid=Q491813|lt=Andhaka|1=Andhaka}} with his {{ill|Trishula|en|Trishula|qid=Q3539698|lt=trident|1=Trishula}}. Note the elephant hide (the skin of {{ill|Gajasura|en|Gajasura|qid=Q5517568|lt=Gajāsura|1=Gajasura}}, whom Shiva also defeated) in one of his arms.|337x337px]] Mahākāla was originally represented in East Asian Buddhist art as a dark-skinned wrathful deity wearing a diadem and a [[Mundamala|necklace of skulls]], with snakes coiled around his neck and arms. One iconographic type portrays him with three heads and six arms, holding a {{ill|Gajasurasamhara|en|Gajasurasamhara|qid=Q12976075|lt=flayed elephant skin|1=Gajasurasamhara}} with his upper hands, a {{ill|Trishula|en|Trishula|qid=Q3539698|lt=trident|1=Trishula}} or a sword horizontally with his lower hands, and a human figure and a goat with his middlehands. Many artworks of this type show Mahākāla in a sitting position, though a description of the deity found in the dictionary compiled by the monk Huilin (慧琳) titled ''[[:en:Yiqiejing Yinyi (Huilin)|The Sound and Meaning of All Sutras]]'' ({{zh|一切經音義|qq=}}, pinyin: ''Yīqièjīng yīnyì'') has him standing on the hands of the earth goddess.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |pages=46–48|qq=}}{{cite web |author1=Huìlín (慧琳) |title=一切經音義 卷十 (Yīqièjīng yīnyì, vol. 10) - T. 2128 |url=https://21dzk.l.u-tokyo.ac.jp/SAT2018/T2128_,54,0366b14.html |website=SAT Daizokyo Text Database |access-date=2021-04-21 |quote=摩訶迦羅　梵語也。唐云大黒天神也。有大神力，壽無量千歳，八臂身青黒雲色，二手懷中横把一三戟叉，右第二手捉一青羖羊，左第二手捉一餓鬼頭髻，右第三手把劔， 左第三手執朅吒𠕀迦（梵語也。是一髑髏幢也。）後二手各於肩上共張一白象皮如披勢，以毒虵貫穿髑髏以爲瓔珞，虎牙上出作大忿怒形，雷電煙火以爲威光身形極大，足下有一地神女天，以兩手承足者也。|qq=}} The same work describes Mahākāla as having eight arms, holding an elephant skin, a trident, a {{ill|Preta|en|Preta|qid=Q1332306|lt=Preta|1=Preta}}, a goat, a sword, and a [[:en:Khatvanga|''khatvāṅga'']] (a skull-topped club or staff). Some images of Mahākāla of this type found in {{ill|Dunhuang|en|Dunhuang|qid=Q319114|lt=Dunhuang|1=Dunhuang}} (dating from the 9th-10th centuries) meanwhile show him standing on a snake. Another iconographic variant (not found in Chinese texts but attested in Japan) depicts Mahākāla with one head and two arms, holding a sword in his right hand and a skull cup (''[[:en:Kapāla|Kapāla]]'') in his left. He is sometimes also shown as trampling on the elephant-headed deity Vināyaka (the Buddhist analogue to the Hindu {{ill|Ganesha|en|Ganesha|qid=Q1579|lt=Ganesha|1=Ganesha}}, though the Buddhist version is sometimes also perceived as a negative figure), another deity Mahākāla is associated with. Indeed, the two deities are shown together in the outer northeast (upper left) corner of the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|qid=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm|1=Womb Realm}} (''Garbhadhātu'') [[Mandala|Maṇḍala]], one of the two main maṇḍalas of East Asian esoteric Buddhism. {{Gallery|align=center |File:Standing Mahākāla (Dunhuang Mogao).jpg|Māhākala holding up an elephant skin and standing on a snake. Detail of a painting found in the {{ill|Mogao Caves|en|Mogao Caves|qid=Q43286|lt=Mogao Caves|1=Mogao Caves}} in {{ill|Dunhuang|en|Dunhuang|qid=Q319114|lt=Dunhuang|1=Dunhuang}}, {{ill|Gansu|en|Gansu|qid=Q42392|lt=Gansu|1=Gansu}}, {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} |File:Mahākāla and Vināyaka.jpg|Two-armed Mahākāla (lower right) holding a sword and a {{ill|Kapala|en|Kapala|qid=Q2658471|lt=skull cup|1=Kapala}} |File:Mahakala (Besson Zakki).jpg|Mahākāla with one head and six arms, from the ''Besson zakki'' (別尊雑記), a Japanese compendium of Buddhist iconography|File:Daikokuten (Kakuzenshō) - 03.jpg|[[Mandala]] of Mahākāla and the {{ill|Matrikas|en|Matrikas|qid=Q1789401|lt=Aṣṭamātṛkas|1=Matrikas}}, from the ''Kakuzenshō'' (覚禅鈔), an early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} iconographic compendium|qq=}} [[File:Cina, mahakala, guardiano della dottrina (dharmapala), nell'aseptto sitacintamani, xviii sec..JPG|thumb|{{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}} statuette of Dàhēitiān. {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}. 17th century.]] Yijing describes the statues of Mahākāla he had seen in Indian monasteries as "holding a golden bag and seated on a small chair, with one foot hanging down towards the ground." Some scholars believe that the images Yijing saw may have actually been that of the god {{ill|Kubera|en|Kubera|qid=Q223617|lt=Kubera|1=Kubera}}, who was represented in Indian art as carrying a money bag; indeed, he identifies 'Mahākāla' as being part of the retinue of the "great god" (大天, i.e. [[Shiva#Etymology and other names|Mahādeva]] / Maheśvara). It is thought that the two gods may have been conflated at some point; images of both deities are commonly found {{ill|Dvarapala|en|Dvarapala|qid=Q919849|lt=guarding the entrances of temples|1=Dvarapala}} in India, Nepal and {{ill|Greater India|en|Greater India|qid=Q11014588|lt=other places influenced by Hindu-Buddhist culture|1=Greater India}}, and Kubera was, as mentioned, closely associated with Shiva. The image of the sack-carrying Daikokuten that would become the standard in Japan is thus thought to be derived from Kubera's iconography.[[File:Mahakala (one of a pair of door guardians), Indonesia, Central Java, 700-900 AD, andesite - Asian Art Museum of San Francisco - DSC01344.JPG|left|thumb|292x292px|Stone sculpture of Mahākāla (one of a pair of {{ill|Dvarapala|en|Dvarapala|qid=Q919849|lt=door guardians|1=Dvarapala}}), from {{ill|Central Java|en|Central Java|qid=Q3557|lt=Central Java|1=Central Java}}, {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}}]]The earliest Japanese representations of Mahākāla-Daikokuten can be classified into two types: one (associated with the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon school|1=Shingon Buddhism}}) shows the deity standing, his left hand holding a sack slung over his shoulder, with his right hand clenched into a fist and resting on the right hip, while the other (associated with the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai school|1=Tendai}}) depicts him as sitting. Most of these images show Daikokuten wearing Japanese clothing, though a few has him wearing armor. The standing portrayal is first mentioned in the 10th-century Shingon work ''Yōson dōjōkan'' (要尊道場観, 'Visualizations of the Ritual Spheres of the Essential Deities') and an apocryphal 11th-century text titled ''Daikokutenjin-hō'' (大黒天神法, 'The Tantra of Mahākāla'), while the seated portrayal's first literary appearance is in the 13th-century ''Asabashō'' (阿娑縛抄), a Tendai iconographical and ritual compendium. The ''Daikokutenjin-hō'' describes Daikokuten as black in color, wearing ''eboshi'' ([[:ja:烏帽子|烏帽子]], a black cap worn by Japanese noblemen), ''kariginu'' ([[:ja:狩衣|狩衣]], informal aristocratic outerwear), and ''{{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|qid=Q164668|lt=Hakama|1=Hakama}}'' (loose, skirt-like trousers), with his right fist resting at his waist and his left hand clutching a large bag, the color of which is that of rat's hair.{{Cite book|last=Uryū|first=Naka|url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/971512631|title=Yoku wakaru Shingon-shū (よくわかる真言宗)|date=2016|publisher=Kadokawa|isbn=978-4-04-400135-3|pages=80–81|oclc=971512631|qq=}}[[File:MET 1984 318.jpeg|thumb|247x247px|{{ill|Kubera|en|Kubera|qid=Q223617|lt=Kubera|1=Kubera}} holding a money bag in his left hand and a bowl in the other, with one foot in the ground]]The oldest surviving examples of the two iconographic variants date from around the 11th century (late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}). The oldest standing Daikokuten statue is found in {{ill|Kanzeon-ji|en|Kanzeon-ji|qid=Q3192777|lt=Kanzeon-ji|1=Kanzeon-ji}} in {{ill|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|en|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|qid=Q728218|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} and depicts him wearing ''eboshi,'' knee-length ''hakama'', and shoes.{{Cite web|title=市内の指定文化財 彫刻|url=http://www.city.dazaifu.lg.jp/bunkazai/shinaibunkazai/shiteibunkazai/6026.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-04-25|website=Dazaifu City Official Website|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180807060709/http://www.city.dazaifu.lg.jp:80/bunkazai/shinaibunkazai/shiteibunkazai/6026.html |archive-date=2018-08-07 |qq=}} The oldest depiction of the sitting Daikokuten, kept in [[:ja:金剛輪寺|Kongōrin-ji]] in {{ill|Echi District, Shiga|en|Echi District, Shiga|qid=Q1131097|lt=Echi District|1=Echi District, Shiga}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, meanwhile, shows him wearing armor, seated on a rock and holding a small bag and a club or staff.{{cite web |title=秘仏日本最古の大黒天（金運の神）特別公開 |url=http://kongourinji.jp/news/index.php?nid=25 |website=Kongōrin-ji Official Website |access-date=2021-04-21 |language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite news |author1=Ishisone, Nodoka (石曽根和花) |title=秘仏の大黒天、期間限定公開 愛荘・金剛輪寺本堂 |url=https://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/162256 |access-date=2021-04-21 |work=Chunichi Shimbun |date=2020-11-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201129202048/https://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/162256|archive-date=2020-11-29 |language=Japanese|qq=}} Daikokuten's iconography evolved during the 14th century onwards, when he increasingly became portrayed as a smiling man with a rotund belly, holding a {{ill|Uchide no kozuchi|en|Uchide no kozuchi|qid=Q7876713|lt=mallet|1=Uchide no kozuchi}} and standing or sitting on rice bales. The origin of the mallet attribute is uncertain, although {{ill|Bernard Faure|en|Bernard Faure|qid=Q2897888|lt=Bernard Faure|1=Bernard Faure}} (2015) links it with Mahākāla-Daikokuten's association with the cult of the {{ill|Matrikas|en|Matrikas|qid=Q1789401|lt=Saptamātṛkas|1=Matrikas}} (the 'Seven Mothers'), who are pictured as holding mallets – symbolizing their role as plague deities – in the Madarijin (摩怛哩神) ritual. During the 16th century (late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}), the three deities Daikokuten, Vaiśravaṇa-Bishamonten and Sarasvatī-Benzaiten were fused together into the three-headed 'Sanmen Daikokuten' (三面大黒天, lit. "Three-Faced Daikokuten"), which in a way 'reconnected' the deity's popular benign form with his less well-known wrathful form. This form was eventually introduced in later variants of the legend of Daikokuten's apparition to Saichō in Mount Hiei: in response to Saichō's dilemma over how to provide daily sustenance for three thousand monks, the god is now said to have shown himself to the latter with three faces and six arms. {{Gallery|align=center |File:Manshu-in Buddhist Temple - Stone statue of Daikokuten.jpg|Armored Daikokuten at [[Manshu-in]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} |File:Daikokuten (Kakuzenshō) - 02.jpg|Daikokuten, from the ''Kakuzenshō''|File:Daikokuten (Kakuzenshō) - 01.jpg|Sitting Daikokuten, from the ''Kakuzenshō'' |File:Standing Daikokuten (Mahakala) by Kaiken, Nanbokucho period, dated 1347 (Jowa 3), wood with polychromy, view 1 - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05070.JPG|Statue of Daikokuten from [[Tōdai-ji]], dated 1347 ({{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|qid=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} 3, [[Nanbokucho period]]); currently at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} |File:三面大黒天 - Sanmen Daikokuten.jpg|Sanmen Daikokuten, a fusion of Daikokuten (center), {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} (left), and [[Benzaiten]] (right), by [[:en:Katsushika Hokusai|Katsushika Hokusai]]|qq=}} [[File:Roku Daikoku (六大黒).jpg|thumb|267x267px|Roku Daikoku, from the [[Butsuzōzui|''Butsuzō Zui'']]]] An iconographic grouping known as the 'Roku Daikoku' (六大黒天, lit. "Six Daikoku") also developed during the same period, showing the deity in six different forms:{{cite web|title=Butsuzōzui (Illustrated Compendium of Buddhist Images)|url=http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|location=Ehime University Library|page=(059.jpg)|language=Japanese|format=digital photos|date=1796|access-date=2016-04-29|archive-date=2018-10-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181010061804/http://www.lib.ehime-u.ac.jp/SUZUKA/316/index.html|url-status=dead|qq=}}{{Cite web|title=六大黒|url=http://tobifudo.jp/butuzo/6daikoku/index.html|access-date=2021-04-28|website=Flying Deity Tobifudō (Ryūkō-zan Shōbō-in Official Website)|qq=}} # '''Biku Daikoku''' (比丘大黒): Daikokuten in the form of a Buddhist monk (''bhikkhu''), holding a mallet in his right hand and a sword in his left # '''Ōji Kara Daikoku''' (王子迦羅大黒): Daikokuten as a prince (王子, ''ōji'') brandishing a sword and a {{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|qid=Q695709|lt=Vajra|1=Vajra}}; sometimes interpreted as Mahākāla-Daikokuten's son{{Cite web|title=王子迦羅大黒|url=http://tobifudo.jp/butuzo/6daikoku/6dai-2.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-04-28|website=Flying Deity Tobifudō (Ryūkō-zan Shōbō-in Official Website)|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100831142106/http://tobifudo.jp:80/butuzo/6daikoku/6dai-2.html |archive-date=2010-08-31 |qq=}} # '''{{ill|Yaksha|en|Yaksha|qid=Q6011093|lt=Yasha|1=Yaksha}} Daikoku''' (夜叉大黒): Daikokuten as the subduer of demons (''yakṣa''), wearing Japanese aristocratic garb and holding a wheel ({{ill|Dharmachakra|en|Dharmachakra|qid=Q665056|lt=''dharmacakra''|1=Dharmachakra}}) in his right hand # '''[[Makakara]] Daikokunyo''' (摩伽迦羅大黒女): Daikokuten as a female figure holding a bale of rice above her head; sometimes interpreted as Mahākāla-Daikokuten's consort (i.e. {{ill|Mahakali|en|Mahakali|qid=Q2866458|lt=Mahākāḷī|1=Mahakali}}) # '''Shinda Daikoku''' (信陀大黒 or 真陀大黒): Daikokuten as a boy with the wish-granting jewel (''[[Cintamani|cintāmaṇi]]'') in his hand # '''Makara Daikoku''' (摩伽羅大黒): Daikokuten in his 'regular' benign form, holding a mallet and a sack The 17th-18th centuries ({{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}) marked the appearance of the cult of the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] ({{lang|ja-latn|Shichifukujin|qq=}}), of which Daikokuten is a key member. Daikokuten's rise in popularity among the common people during the late medieval and early modern periods led to the god becoming a popular subject in art. File:Daikoku with Rats by Kawanabe Kyosai.jpg|Daikokuten with rats pulling a radish ''[[mikoshi]]'', by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|en|Kawanabe Kyōsai|qid=Q2838030|lt=Kawanabe Kyōsai|1=Kawanabe Kyōsai}} File:Ebisu daikoku no manzai LCCN2009615278.tif|Ebisu and Daikokuten, by [[:ja:玉川舟調|Tamagawa Shūchō]] File:MET 91 1 934 O2 sf.jpg|Ivory ''[[netsuke]]'' of Daikokuten with mallet and rat File:Okimono with Daikoku-ten, Japan, Edo period, 1864, earthenware - Royal Ontario Museum - DSC04286.JPG|''{{ill|Okimono|en|Okimono|qid=Q750735|lt=Okimono|1=Okimono}}'' in the shape of Daikokuten's hammer File:Bank of Japan silver convertible one yen banknote 1885.jpg|{{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}}-era {{ill|1 yen note|en|1 yen note|qid=Q4596054|lt=1 yen banknote|1=1 yen note}} featuring Daikokuten, by Italian engraver {{ill|Edoardo Chiossone|en|Edoardo Chiossone|qid=Q973922|lt=Edoardo Chiossone|1=Edoardo Chiossone}} File:Daikokuten Taipei.jpg|Statue of Daikokuten in {{ill|Taipei|en|Taipei|qid=Q1867|lt=Taipei|1=Taipei}} ==Worship== [[File:BonjiMa.png|thumb|130px|म (''ma''), Mahākāla's seed syllable ([[:en:Bījā|''bīja'']]) in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|qid=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ script|1=Siddhaṃ script}}|left]] [[File:Daikokuten Statue at Kanda Myojin.jpg|thumb|290px|Statue of Ōkuninushi as Daikokuten in [[Kanda Shrine]] (Kanda Myōjin) in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}]] The god continues to enjoy an exalted position as a deity of fortune and the household in Japan. Images of Daikokuten can be found in both Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines in the country (a relic of the long-standing fusion of the two religions), though in the latter case, these are usually interpreted and revered as representations of the Japanese god Ōkuninushi rather than the Buddhist Mahākāla.{{Cite web|title=出雲大社の信仰|url=https://izumotaisha-saitama.com/izumo-shinko/|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-06|website=出雲大社埼玉分院 (Izumo Taisha Saitama Branch)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200814004426/https://izumotaisha-saitama.com/izumo-shinko/ |archive-date=2020-08-14 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=えんむすびの大神について|url=http://kameoka-izumo.or.jp/about.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-06|website=出雲大社京都分院 (Izumo Taisha Kyoto Branch)|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100706165226/http://kameoka-izumo.or.jp:80/about.html |archive-date=2010-07-06 |qq=}} Due to his association with rats, days under the zodiac sign of the {{ill|Rat (zodiac)|en|Rat (zodiac)|qid=Q721997|lt=Rat|1=Rat (zodiac)}} (子日, ''ne-no-hi''), especially that of the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|qid=Q246658|lt=Yang Wood Rat|1=Sexagenary cycle}} (甲子, ''kōshi'' / ''kinoe-ne'') are considered to be sacred to Mahākāla-Daikokuten (and by extension, Ōkuninushi), with the first (初甲子, ''hatsu kōshi'') and last ''kōshi'' days (納め甲子, ''osame kōshi'') of a given year being especially held in great esteem. Special ceremonies and festivals are held on these days at many places of worship dedicated to the deity.{{Cite book|last1=Sun|first1=Ruth Q.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kZHTAgAAQBAJ|title=Asian Animal Zodiac|last2=Sun|first2=Norma|date=2012|publisher=Tuttle Publishing|isbn=978-1-4629-0371-9|language=en|qq=}}{{Cite web|last=|title=本光寺の仏神|url=https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/18076|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=まるごとe! ちば|publisher=千葉県観光物産協会 (Chiba Prefectural Tourism and Local Products Association)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210508160423/https://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/18076 |archive-date=2021-05-08 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=大黒天|url=http://www.fukagawa7.net/mo/daikoku/index.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=深川七福神ホームページ|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201101112320/http://www.fukagawa7.net/mo/daikoku/index.html |archive-date=2020-11-01 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=甲子祭案内|url=http://matugasaki-daikokuten.net/kinoenesai.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=Matsugasaki Daikokuten Official Website|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140811065554/http://matugasaki-daikokuten.net/kinoenesai.html |archive-date=2014-08-11 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=甲子祭|url=http://www.nakanotake.com/kino|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=Myōgi-san Nakanotake Shrine (妙義山 中之嶽神社)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190115052338/http://www.nakanotake.com/kino |archive-date=2019-01-15 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=甲子（きのえね）祭・甲子秋まつり|url=https://www.city.fuji.shizuoka.jp/fujijikan/enjoy/kb719c00000006mc.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=富士じかん (www.city.fuji.shizuoka.jp)|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150302052324/http://www.city.fuji.shizuoka.jp:80/fujijikan/enjoy/kb719c00000006mc.html |archive-date=2015-03-02 |qq=}} During the early modern period, Daikokuten's association with wealth and prosperity precipitated a custom known as ''fukunusubi'', or "theft of fortune". This custom started with the belief that whoever stole divine figures was assured of good fortune if not caught in the act. The ''toshi-no-ichi'' (year-end market) held at [[Sensō-ji]] in [[Asakusa]] became the main venue of the sale and disposal of such images by the fortune-seekers. Many small stalls were opened where articles including images of Daikokuten were sold on the eve of New Year celebrations.{{cite book|last=Thakur|first=Upendra|title=Some Aspects of Asian History and Culture|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=m42TldA_OvAC&pg=PA190|year=1986|publisher=Abhinav Publications|isbn=978-81-7017-207-9|page=190|qq=}}{{Cite web|last=|first=|title=浅草市 (Asakusa-ichi)|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%B5%85%E8%8D%89%E5%B8%82-2001545|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-06|website=コトバンク (Kotobank)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210506155736/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E6%B5%85%E8%8D%89%E5%B8%82-2001545 |archive-date=2021-05-06 |qq=}}{{Cite web|last=|first=|title=大黒 (Daikoku)|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E9%BB%92-556961|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-06|website=コトバンク (Kotobank)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171023233654/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E9%BB%92-556961 |archive-date=2017-10-23 |qq=}} Another practice known as ''tsubute'' (礫, lit. "stone throwing") involved 'stealing' the wealth from a rich house by throwing into it a Daikokuten talisman at the [[Rat (zodiac)#Hour of the Rat|hour of the rat]] (around midnight). An esoteric ritual performed in many Tendai temples where Daikokuten is worshiped known as ''yokubei-ku'' (浴餅供, lit. "{{ill|Glutinous rice|en|Glutinous rice|qid=Q115443|lt=Glutinous rice|1=Glutinous rice}} bath") involves {{ill|Abhisheka|en|Abhisheka|qid=Q75799|lt=pouring|1=Abhisheka}} {{ill|Congee|en|Congee|qid=Q878624|lt=rice porridge|1=Congee}} over a statue of the deity.{{cite book |last1=Miyata |first1=Noboru |title=七福神信仰事典 (Shichifukujin shinkō jiten) |date=1998 |publisher=Ebisu Kōshō Shuppan |page=17 |isbn=9784900901063 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-h-1ITXgQnIC&dq=%E6%B5%B4%E9%A4%85%E4%BE%9B+%E5%A4%A7%E9%BB%92%E5%A4%A9&pg=PA17 |language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Fuji |first1=Tatsuhiko |title=決定版 呪法全書―知られざる"秘伝の祈祷"を全公開！ |date=2012 |publisher=Gakken Plus |isbn=978-4054052789 |pages=108–109|language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=大黒天供養の秘法「浴餅」とは？ |url=https://www.butuzou-world.com/column/miyazawa/20200225-2/ |website=仏像ワールド（Butuzou World） |date=25 February 2020 |access-date=2021-06-27|language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=年中行事 |url=http://www.isakiji.jp/nentyugyouji.html |website=天台宗 伊崎寺 (Isaki-ji Official Website) |access-date=2021-06-27|language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=孝道山で｢春の浴餅供｣ |url=https://www.townnews.co.jp/0117/2020/02/20/518241.html |website=タウンニュース（神奈川県全域・東京多摩地域の地域情報紙） |date=20 February 2020 |access-date=2021-06-27|language=Japanese|qq=}} ===Bījā and mantra=== The ''[[bīja]]'' or seed syllable used to represent Mahākāla-Daikokuten in Japanese esoteric Buddhism is '''{{IAST|ma|qq=}}''' (म), written in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|qid=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ script|1=Siddhaṃ script}}.{{cite web |title=大黒天 (Daikokuten) |url=http://tobifudo.jp/butuzo/7god/daikoku.html |website=Flying Deity Tobifudō (Ryūkō-zan Shōbō-in Official Website) |access-date=2021-04-10|qq=}} Mahākāla's {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}} meanwhile is as follows: {| class="wikitable" |+ !Sanskrit !Japanese (romanized) !Chinese characters !{{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|qid=Q48332|lt=Hiragana|1=Hiragana}} |- |''Oṃ Mahākālāya [[:en:Svāhā|Svāhā]]'' |''On Makakyaraya sowaka'' |唵 摩訶迦羅耶 娑婆訶 |おん まかきゃらや そわか |} === Temples === [[File:東叡山寛永寺護国院・大黒天.jpg|thumb|355x355px|image of Daikokuten at Gokoku-in, Taitō, Tokyo]] [[File:朝日開運大黒天.jpg|thumb|300px|Asahi Kaiun Daikokuten at Mano-dera, Minamibōsō, Chiba Prefecture]] [[File:Statue of Daikokuten, Kōshō-ji.jpg|thumb|Sanmen Daikokuten at {{ill|Kōshō-ji (Uji)|en|Kōshō-ji (Uji)|qid=Q11613456|lt=Kōshō-ji|1=Kōshō-ji (Uji)}}, {{ill|Uji|en|Uji|qid=Q326956|lt=Uji|1=Uji}}, Kyoto]] The following are a few examples of Buddhist temples that either have Daikokuten as their main focus of worship (''[[honzon]]'') or enshrine him in an auxiliary capacity. * '''[[Enryaku-ji]]''' ({{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|qid=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu|1=Ōtsu}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}) – {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} :The Daikoku-dō (大黒堂, "Hall of Daikoku") located in the temple complex enshrines an image of the three-faced Sanmen Daikokuten attributed to Saichō, who is said to have made it after the god appeared to him and promised to become the patron of his monastic community.{{cite web |title=比叡山三面大黒天縁起 |url=https://www.hieizan.or.jp/wp-content/themes/enryakuji/pdf/daikoku_engi.pdf |website=Hiei-zan Enryaku-ji Official Website |access-date=2021-05-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} * '''[[:ja:大黒寺 (羽曳野市)|Daikoku-ji]] (大黒寺)''' ({{ill|Habikino|en|Habikino|qid=Q694308|lt=Habikino City|1=Habikino}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}) – [[Sōtō|Sōtō Zen]] :One of the contenders for the birthplace of the cult of Mahākāla-Daikokuten in Japan. The temple's founding story claims that it was first established in 665 CE by the ascetic {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}} after seeing a vision of Daikokuten riding a five-colored cloud.{{cite web |title=大黒寺のいわれ |url=http://daikokuji.kt.fc2.com/sub2.htm |website=Daikoku-ji (Habikino) Official Website |access-date=2021-05-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} * '''[[:ja:大黒寺 (京都市伏見区)|Daikoku-ji]]''' ({{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) – {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} ([[Tō-ji|Tōji-ha]]) :Said to have been established by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}, the founder of Shingon Buddhism, to whom is also attributed the image of Daikokuten that serve as the temple's ''honzon''. Originally named '''Chōfuku-ji''' (長福寺), the temple was renamed in 1615 after [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]] designated it as a "prayer center" (祈願所, ''kigansho'') for his clan, the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|qid=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu|1=Shimazu clan}}, and their fiefdom, {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}, where religious services are performed on their behalf.{{cite web |title=大黒寺 |url=https://ja.kyoto.travel/tourism/single02.php?Category_id=9&tourism_id=419 |website=京都観光NAVI |publisher=Kyoto City Tourism Association |access-date=2021-05-08 |language=Japanese|qq=}} *'''[[:ja:妙円寺 (京都市)|Myōen-ji]] (妙円寺)''' (Matsugasaki Higashimachi, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Kyoto) – {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|qid=Q206611|lt=Nichiren-shū|1=Nichiren-shū}} :Founded in 1616; also known as '''Matsugasaki Daikokuten''' (松ヶ崎大黒天). Enshrines a statue of Daikokuten attributed to Saichō.{{Cite web|title=大黒天|url=http://matugasaki-daikokuten.net/daikokuten.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=Matsugasaki Daikokuten Official Website|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140811074421/http://matugasaki-daikokuten.net/daikokuten.html |archive-date=2014-08-11 |qq=}} * '''[[:ja:真野寺|Mano-dera / Mano-ji]] (真野寺)''' (Kubo, {{ill|Minamibōsō|en|Minamibōsō|qid=Q873521|lt=Minamibōsō|1=Minamibōsō}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}) – Shingon ({{ill|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|en|Shingon-shū Chisan-ha|qid=Q674374|lt=Chisan-ha|1=Shingon-shū Chisan-ha}}) :This temple, founded by {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} in 725 CE and dedicated to the Thousand-Armed {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Avalokiteśvara|1=Avalokiteśvara}} ([[Kannon|Senju Kannon]]), enshrines an image of Daikokuten claimed to be the work of the Tendai priest {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|qid=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}}, known as the ' Kaiun Daikokuten' (朝日開運大黒天) – so named because Ennin is claimed to have carved it in the year 860 after witnessing a vision of the god at daybreak, as the sun was rising.{{cite web |title=縁起 |url=https://www.manodaikoku.com/manoji-engi |website=Mano-dera Official Website |access-date=2021-05-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} * '''[[:ja:英信寺|Eishin-ji]] (英信寺)''' (Shitaya, {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|qid=Q232641|lt=Taitō City|1=Taitō}}, Tokyo) – [[Jōdo-shū]] :A temple to the buddha [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyorai) established in 1631. A statue of Sanmen Daikokuten attributed to Kūkai is enshrined in the Daikoku-dō beside the temple's main hall.{{cite web |title=下谷 英信寺（えいしんじ） |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xeisin.htm |website=天空仙人の神社仏閣めぐり |access-date=2021-05-07|qq=}}{{cite web |title=英信寺 |url=https://tokyo-trip.org/spot/visiting/tk0521/ |website=東京とりっぷ |date=31 December 2019 |access-date=2021-05-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=英信寺 |url=https://t-navi.city.taito.lg.jp/spot/tabid90.html?pdid1=436 |website=TAITOおでかけナビ|access-date=2021-05-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}} * '''[[:ja:護国院 (台東区)|Gokoku-in]] (護国院)''' (Taitō City, Tokyo) – Tendai :Part of the {{ill|Ueno Park|en|Ueno Park|qid=Q746216|lt=Ueno Park|1=Ueno Park}}-[[Kan'ei-ji]] temple complex. Enshrines a painting of Daikokuten attributed to {{ill|Fujiwara no Nobuzane|en|Fujiwara no Nobuzane|qid=Q3090653|lt=Fujiwara no Nobuzane|1=Fujiwara no Nobuzane}} donated to the temple by {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|qid=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}}.{{cite web |title=護国院 |url=https://tokyo-trip.org/spot/visiting/tk0516/ |website=東京とりっぷ |date=27 December 2019 |access-date=2021-05-07 |language=Japanese|qq=}}{{cite web |title=東叡山寛永寺 護國院（護国院｜通称：護國院大黒天） |url=http://www.tendaitokyo.jp/jiinmei/gokokuin/ |website=天台宗東京教区 (Tendai-shū Tokyo Parish) |access-date=2021-05-07|qq=}} * '''Kyōō-ji (経王寺)''' (Haramachi, {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|qid=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku City|1=Shinjuku}}, Tokyo) – Nichiren-shū :Founded in 1598 by the monk Nichijō, who installed there an image of Daikokuten from {{ill|Kuon-ji|en|Kuon-ji|qid=Q1516881|lt=Kuon-ji|1=Kuon-ji}} said to have been carved by Nichiren's disciple [[:ja:日法|Nippō]].{{cite web |title=秘仏・開運大黒天像 |url=https://ameblo.jp/kyoouji/entry-12389845941.html |website=Kyoouji Official Blog |access-date=2021-05-07|qq=}} * '''Daihō-ji (大法寺)''' ({{ill|Moto-Azabu|en|Moto-Azabu|qid=Q6917930|lt=Moto-Azabu|1=Moto-Azabu}}, {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|qid=Q190088|lt=Minato City|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, Tokyo) – Nichiren-shū :Founded in 1597. The image of Daikokuten kept in this temple, known as 'Sanshin Gusoku Daikoku-sonten' (三神具足大黒尊天), features the deity with the attributes of Benzaiten (hairstyle) and Bishamonten (armor).{{Cite web|date=2020-05-29|title=The god with the best smile in Japan at Daihoji Temple|url=http://visit-minato-city.com/azabu-juban-temple-of-daikokuten-god/|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-09|website=Visit Minato City|language=en-US|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200531161545/http://visit-minato-city.com:80/azabu-juban-temple-of-daikokuten-god/ |archive-date=2020-05-31 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=大法寺・大黒天|url=https://jin3.jp/7fukujin/daikoku.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-09|website=港七福神|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130122004053/http://jin3.jp/7fukujin/daikoku.html |archive-date=2013-01-22 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=麻布十番を中心にめぐる 港七福神 ＋ 宝船めぐり|url=https://www.azabujuban.or.jp/topics/topics_event/7703/|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-09|website=麻布十番商店街 - Azabu-Jūban Shōtengai|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150321193015/http://www.azabujuban.or.jp/topics/topics_event/7703/ |archive-date=2015-03-21 |qq=}} *'''[[:ja:甲子大黒天本山|Hōju-ji]] (宝珠寺)''' – (Onogawamachi, {{ill|Yonezawa, Yamagata|en|Yonezawa, Yamagata|qid=Q734378|lt=Yonezawa City|1=Yonezawa, Yamagata}}, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}) – Shingon ([[Daigo-ji|Daigo-ha]]) :Popularly known as '''Kinoe-ne Daikokuten Honzan''' (甲子大黒天本山). The image of Daikokuten in this temple is attributed to Kūkai and was originally from Dainichi-ji ([[:ja:湯殿山神社 (西川町大井沢)|大日寺]], in modern {{ill|Nishikawa, Yamagata|en|Nishikawa, Yamagata|qid=Q1348988|lt=Nishikawa|1=Nishikawa, Yamagata}}, {{ill|Nishimurayama District, Yamagata|en|Nishimurayama District, Yamagata|qid=Q1155585|lt=Nishimurayama District|1=Nishimurayama District, Yamagata}}), one of the four ''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=bettō-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}'' (administrative temples) of {{ill|Yudonosan Shrine|en|Yudonosan Shrine|qid=Q11563607|lt=Yudonosan Shrine|1=Yudonosan Shrine}}, one of the {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|qid=Q1155726|lt=Three Sacred Mountains|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}} of {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}} (Dewa Sanzan). The statue was transferred to its current location after Dainichi-ji was converted into a Shinto shrine during the Meiji period.{{Cite web|title=４．大黒さまについて|url=http://www.daikoku-sama.com/daikoku4.htm|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=Kinoe-ne Daikokuten Honzan Homepage|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20011219102216/http://www.daikoku-sama.com:80/daikoku4.htm |archive-date=2001-12-19 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=宝寿の湯の由来・大黒天|url=https://www.hojunoyu.com/daikoku.html|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=小野川温泉 宝寿の湯|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210508172552/https://www.hojunoyu.com/daikoku.html |archive-date=2021-05-08 |qq=}}{{Cite web|title=神仏習合；神仏分離前の出羽三山|url=http://tyokyojin.sakura.ne.jp/180720.html|access-date=2021-05-08|website=tyokyojin.sakura.ne.jp|qq=}}{{Cite web|date=2019-04-15|title=鈴の宿 登府屋旅館|url=https://www.tabino-yado.com/hotels/%e9%88%b4%e3%81%ae%e5%ae%bf%e3%80%80%e7%99%bb%e5%ba%9c%e5%b1%8b%e6%97%85%e9%a4%a8/|access-date=2021-05-08|website=旅宿ジャパン|language=ja|qq=}} {{Gallery|align=center|title=Daikokuten temples |File:Enryakuji syussedaikokudo.jpg|Daikoku-dō (大黒堂) at the [[Enryaku-ji]] temple complex in {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}, the headquarters of the Tendai school |File:Daikoku-ji Kyoto 01.JPG|Daikoku-ji (Fushimi-ku, Kyoto) |File:Hoshakuji03s2040.jpg|Kozuchi Shrine (小槌宮, ''Kozuchi-no-miya'') at [[:ja:宝積寺|Hōshaku-ji]] ({{ill|Ōyamazaki, Kyoto|en|Ōyamazaki, Kyoto|qid=Q450287|lt=Ōyamazaki|1=Ōyamazaki, Kyoto}}, Kyoto) |File:Daikokuji (Habikino, Osaka)1.jpg|Daikoku-ji (Habikino, Osaka) |File:Myôgon-ji Temple - Daikoku-dô.jpg|Daikoku-dō at {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|qid=Q3080596|lt=Myōgon-ji|1=Toyokawa Inari}} (Toyokawa Inari) in {{ill|Toyokawa, Aichi|en|Toyokawa, Aichi|qid=Q389495|lt=Toyokawa|1=Toyokawa, Aichi}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, famous for its guardian deity [[Dakini|Toyokawa Dakiniten]] |File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikoku-dō at Toyokawa Inari's Tokyo branch temple in {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|qid=Q416131|lt=Akasaka|1=Akasaka, Tokyo}} |File:Kaneiji gokokuin.jpg|Gokoku-in ({{ill|Ueno Park|en|Ueno Park|qid=Q746216|lt=Ueno Park|1=Ueno Park}}) |File:SMALL TEMPLE IN TOKYO NEAR ZOO AND LAKE.jpg|Daikokuten-dō (大黒天堂) at {{ill|Shinobazu Pond|en|Shinobazu Pond|qid=Q5365093|lt=Shinobazu Pond|1=Shinobazu Pond}}, Ueno Park |File:中山法華経寺 - panoramio (19).jpg|Setsu-dō (刹堂) at {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|qid=Q786113|lt=Nakayama Hokekyō-ji|1=Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)}} (Ichikawa, Chiba), enshrining [[:en:Hārītī|Hārītī]] (Kishimojin), the {{ill|Ten Rākṣasīs|en|Ten Rākṣasīs|qid=Q11405165|lt=Ten Rakṣasīs|1=Ten Rākṣasīs}}, and Daikokuten |File:真野寺-大黒天.jpg|Mano-dera (Minamibōsō, Chiba)|qq=}} {{Gallery|align=center|title=Daikokuten images in Shinto shrines |File:Jishu-jinja Shintô Shrine - Nade-Daikoku.jpg|Nade Daikoku (撫で大国), a touchable image of Daikokuten (identified here with Ōkuninushi) at [[:ja:地主神社|Jishu Shrine]] (Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto) |File:Okunimushino-Mikoto in Jishu-jinja.JPG|Ōkuninushi bearing Daikokuten's mallet, Jishu Shrine |File:Izumo-Taisha-Kyoto-bun%27in_Daikoku.jpg|Statue of Daikokuten-Ōkuninushi at [[:ja:出雲大社京都分院|Izumo Taisha Kyoto Bun'in]] (branch of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in {{ill|Kameoka, Kyoto|en|Kameoka, Kyoto|qid=Q633996|lt=Kameoka|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}}, Kyoto) |File:新日吉神宮_-_大黒様.jpg|Statue of 'Daikoku-sama' (大黒様) at [[:ja:新日吉神宮|Imahie Jingū]] (Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto) }} == In popular culture == One theory claims that the term ''daikoku-bashira'' (大黒柱), referring to the central supporting pillar of a [[Housing in Japan#Traditional homes|traditional Japanese house]], originated from Daikokuten's name. This word has also come to figuratively refer to the chief breadwinner of a family.{{Cite web|date=2005-05-23|title=「大黒柱」の意味・由来・語源|url=https://gogen-yurai.jp/daikokubashira/|access-date=2021-05-08|website=語源由来辞典|language=ja|qq=}}{{Cite web|date=2017-07-20|title=家づくりの豆知識 知ってびっくり、身近な建築由来の言葉たち {{!|qq=}}|url=https://tophomes.co.jp/kotsu/etymology/|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=株式会社トップホームズ (TOP HOMES)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200803145128/https://tophomes.co.jp/kotsu/etymology/ |archive-date=2020-08-03 |qq=}} The wife of a Buddhist monk was also referred to in popular slang as ''Daikoku,'' due to Daikokuten's association with the kitchen and the household in general. A traditional art widely performed during the medieval and early modern periods known as ''Daikoku-mai'' (大黒舞, lit. "dance of Daikoku") involved performers – usually social outcasts (''{{ill|Hinin|en|Hinin|qid=Q3135765|lt=Hinin|1=Hinin}}'') – costumed as Daikokuten going from door to door to dance and sing in exchange for donations.{{Cite book|last=Lancashire|first=Terence A.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ph0GDAAAQBAJ|title=An Introduction to Japanese Folk Performing Arts|date=2016|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-317-18169-9|pages=93–94|language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book|last=Hearn|first=Lafcadio|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qzsQAAAAYAAJ|title=Kokoro: Hints and Echoes of Japanese Inner Life|date=1896|publisher=Houghton, Mifflin|isbn=978-0-7222-2533-2|pages=330–334|language=en|qq=}}{{Cite web|last=|first=|title=大黒舞 (Daikoku-mai)|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E9%BB%92%E8%88%9E-556975|url-status=live|access-date=2021-05-08|website=コトバンク (Kotobank)|language=ja|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200921030252/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E9%BB%92%E8%88%9E-556975 |archive-date=2020-09-21 |qq=}} Figurines or plaques of his image can be found in shops throughout Japan.{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2008 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-533262-9
